title: Fantasy Adventuring Gear
description: The everyday equipment of people who go into dangerous places — not magical items but the well-made, the practical, the modified, and the improvised. The gear that tells you something about who carries it.

entries.result: A tinderbox with a personal modification: the flint has been angled for left-handed striking and the tinder compartment has been lined with waxed cloth to keep the tinder dry.
A rope of fifty feet, silk, much more expensive than hemp and much lighter, with a knot tied at every ten feet and a personal mark at the middle.
A grappling hook with collapsible tines, designed to fold flat for travel and spring open on deployment, showing several cycles of use.
A set of iron spikes in a canvas pouch, each stamped with a maker's mark and each the same length, suggesting they were bought for a specific purpose.
A lantern with a directional hood: one side is open, three are shuttered, allowing a focused beam of light rather than an omnidirectional one.
A crowbar with a leather-wrapped grip and a flattened tip that has been filed specifically to fit between a type of door and frame common in old stonework.
A waterproof bag made from sealed oiled leather that will float fully packed and can double as a flotation device for someone who is not fully packed.
A small mirror on a telescoping handle, the kind used for looking around corners, with a cloth case worn from use.
A chalk case containing six sticks of chalk in three colors, plus a short explanation, in the owner's hand, of what each color means in the marking system they use.
A set of pitons and a hammer with a soft face, designed to drive the pitons quietly, with a cord attached to the hammer for one-handed retrieval.
A hunting trap of the leg-hold variety, modified with a pressure plate from a different trap, creating a hybrid that the original manufacturers would not endorse.
A portable writing desk — a hinged board with a felt surface, a shallow tray for ink and quills — that folds to the size of a thick book.
A climbing harness made from leather and rope, worn but sound, with carabiner-style clips that work even with gloves on.
A flask of oil — lamp oil, technically — carried in a purpose-made metal flask that doesn't tip and doesn't leak, because someone learned this the hard way.
A set of lockpicks in a leather roll, six picks plus two tension tools, with one pick slot empty in a way that suggests the missing one was lost recently.
A compass in a brass case with a cracked crystal, still functional, with a correction factor noted in grease pencil on the inside of the lid.
A blanket treated with lanolin for waterproofing, heavier than a standard blanket, worn at one corner, and personal in a way that suggests it's been many places.
A ten-foot pole, collapsible in three sections, threaded brass fittings, that assembles into a usable probe without wobble and disassembles for a pack.
A mess kit in a dented tin: nested pot, pan, cup, and fork-spoon combination, with a fire-blackened exterior and a clean interior because someone has priorities.
A signal whistle on a cord, loud enough to be heard at a quarter mile, with a tone that carries through forest and stone better than a voice.
A set of caltrops, a dozen, in a drawstring bag, each the right size to be ignored until stepped on and then impossible to ignore.
A hourglass in a brass cage, thirty minutes, that travels safely in a padded pouch and that has been used as a timing device in situations where a clock would be conspicuous.
A whetstone in a leather case with a small bottle of honing oil pressed into the same case, used regularly, worn smooth.
A set of weighted throwing balls — not weapons, balance training — carried by someone who practices daily and who has a number of practice marks worn into the bag.
A folding shovel with a blade that locks for use and collapses for travel, adapted from an original design by someone who found the original too heavy.
A waterskin of unusual capacity — two gallons — made from a specific kind of skin that doesn't add flavor to the water, which is why it cost what it cost.
A document tube, brass, waterproofed with wax at the seams, designed to float, and containing, currently, a map that is blank and meant to be filled in.
A coil of wire — thin, strong, non-ferrous — in a range of gauges, used for snares, repairs, and needs that the carrier hasn't explained in the notes they've made about it.
A tent of the quick-pitch variety, four poles, designed for one person who has done it many times, usable by two who have not.
A collection of small bells in a felt pouch, each on a cord, used as improvised alarm systems in positions the carrier intends to sleep near.
A set of vials — a dozen, stoppered, labeled in shorthand — half full of various liquids whose labels require knowledge of the owner's personal notation system.
A belt with hidden compartments: three narrow slots for folded documents, one wider pocket behind the buckle, accessible only when the buckle is removed.
A climbing bag with a waist strap that can be cinched one-handed, worn so that essential items can be reached without taking the pack off.
A personal journal in a waterproof cover, half full, with the last entry three days ago, and a pocket inside the front cover holding a folded document.
A pair of knee pads, leather-fronted, stuffed with horsehair, for sustained work in stone corridors where the cost of kneeling becomes apparent over time.
A repair kit for leather goods: needles, waxed thread, awl, leather patches, and a note tucked in that says 'the patches on the right side of the pouch are the better ones.'
A small folding saw, crosscut, designed for cutting rope or light wood in conditions where a large saw would be impractical.
A compass that also functions as a sundial — the gnomon folds down for travel — used for navigation by someone who doesn't trust a single method.
A fire-resistant glove, right hand only, for handling hot materials, coal, or the particular hazards of someone whose work involves open flame at close range.
A waterproofed case for a tinderbox that is, itself, a tinderbox — two nested waterproof layers and three methods of producing flame in case two fail.
A portable scale, balance type, with a set of standard weights in a felt roll, accurate to a quarter grain, used for someone whose work requires precision.
A pack frame of lightweight wood and leather that carries weight on the hips rather than the shoulders, uncomfortable to wear in a fight but transformatively useful on a long carry.
A set of marked sticks for setting snares, each marked with notches indicating which game the configuration is set for.
A hand lens on a cord, quality glass, used for reading small text, examining details, and on one notable occasion, starting a fire.
A set of stakes and cord for establishing camp perimeter, with a specific layout annotated in a diagram on the inside of the stake bag.
A torch holder that clips to a pack strap, allowing hands-free carrying of a lit torch at shoulder height, which is a better position than it sounds.
A set of needles in graduated sizes for canvas work, sail repair, and wound closure — the last use not advertised but understood.
A short pry bar, flat, designed to fit in a boot top or alongside a pack frame rather than being carried separately.
A personal first aid kit with bandages, wound closures, a suturing needle with thread, and two specific medicinal preparations whose labels say what they're for and how much to use.
A fire steel and two backup flints in a case that also contains dry tinder in a waxed inner compartment, because the owner doesn't trust a single method.
A folding cup that doesn't leak, the result of two previous cups that did, carried in a specific pocket because the owner has learned where things live.
A set of weighted throwing spikes — six, identical, balanced — that are legal in most jurisdictions and more useful than people expect from something that small.
A rain cape of waxed canvas with a drawstring hood, oversized so it covers pack and person, with ties that let it be cinched tight or left full.
A set of survey flags — small, on short stakes, in three colors — used for marking terrain, establishing lines of sight, and the owner's personal mapping notation system.
A personal cook kit with spices in labeled vials, because the owner decided long ago that eating well on the road is achievable and worth the weight.
A boot knife sheath modified to also hold a small fire steel, because the owner concluded that having either be inaccessible was the same problem.
A length of heavy chain, four feet, with a clip at each end, used for securing doors, securing animals, securing people, and occasionally for weight in a bag.
A fishing kit in a small case: hooks in three sizes, weights, a small amount of line, a collapsible rod section, and flies that suggest the owner knows what they're doing.
A small brazier of folded iron — camping, portable — with a windbreak that attaches, used for warmth and cooking in the open.
A set of weather-recording instruments: a small barometer, a wind vane on a collapsible mast, a rain gauge — for someone who plans around the weather rather than reacts to it.
A harness for climbing with built-in positions for specific tools — the loops and pockets arranged by the owner for their specific kit.
A document wallet, oiled leather, that opens to four interior pockets for papers, maps, and documents that should not be mixed up.
A set of ear plugs of waxed wool, which are useful for sleeping in loud places and occasionally for other things entirely.
A folding stool of canvas and light wood that sets up in seconds and can be carried on a pack frame without significant inconvenience.
A vial of contact cement — strong, quick-setting — used for field repairs to leather, wood, and one documented emergency involving stonework.
A small kit of carpenter's tools: a small plane, a marking gauge, a folding square — for someone who thinks in terms of fit and precision.
A wool blanket, full size, that compresses to a quarter of its expanded size and that is warm enough for conditions considerably colder than it looks capable of.
A pair of dark glass lenses in a wire frame — not prescription, shade — for conditions where the sun on snow or sand is a hazard.
A set of trail markers: small metal tabs in two shapes, used to mark route for anyone following, with the two shapes meaning two different things in the owner's system.
A coil of fishing line — strong enough to be used as a trip wire — in a quantity that suggests the owner knows this and bought accordingly.
A light hammer and twenty nails in a belt pouch, carried by someone for whom the occasional need to nail something in place is reliable enough to justify the weight.
A personal lantern of the dark-lantern variety, with a sliding shutter for total darkness, designed so the shutter can be operated with one hand while the other holds a weapon.
A waterproofed pack with a drain plug at the bottom, for carrying things through water crossings or in persistent rain — designed by someone who lost something important to moisture.
A set of glass vials with ground-glass stoppers, a dozen, empty, carried by someone who collects samples of things they encounter.
A piece of chalk wrapped in cloth, each end a different color because the owner's marking system requires two colors and they dislike carrying a case.
A set of small mirrors on adjustable clips — for establishing line-of-sight signals over short distances in terrain where voices carry poorly.
A spare boot, left foot only, in the same style as what the owner wears, which seems pessimistic until it becomes necessary.
A water filter of sand and charcoal in a leather cylinder, simple, heavy, requiring time, turning any running water into something drinkable.
A personal weapon-maintenance kit: oil, cloth, a small file, a leather strop — all sized for whatever weapons the owner uses, fitted together in a case that closes perfectly.
A set of candles in a padded case — eight, all the same diameter, all the same length — with a holder designed to use them without dripping on the surface below.
A field expedient camp kit: groundcloth, ridgeline, tie points — everything needed to make a shelter from the rope and cloth and stakes in the pack, without a tent.
A small illustrated guide to regional plant life, hand-drawn, identifying the edible, the poisonous, and the useful, for a specific region the owner expected to travel through.
A pair of thick-soled sandals for hot environments — worn outside of boots, essentially a sandal over a boot — designed for rock and sand that makes regular footwear impractical.
A length of metal wire pre-cut to twelve inches and packed in a leather tube — for snares, for improvised repairs, for purposes that seem more clear once the owner's notes are read.
A hand-drawn map of a specific location — one building, one cave complex, one city block — made by the owner with annotations that only make sense in context.
A set of small sample bottles with tightly fitting corks, a dozen, used by someone who needs to transport materials without mixing them or losing them.
An emergency ration — dense, caloric, sealed in wax — intended to sustain one person for one day in the event that everything else has gone wrong.
A sleeping mask of folded cloth on an elastic cord, for sleeping when light is unavoidable, worn by someone who decided long ago that sleep quality is a competitive advantage.

A small leather notebook with blank pages, used as a personal log, the first thirty pages filled with a record of places visited, observations made, and assessments delivered in a shorthand that covers the facts without editorializing.
A multi-tool of the folded-blade variety: small knife, can opener, awl, flat screwdriver, file — each blade stamped with a maker's mark from a smith who doesn't make them anymore.

A set of wound dressings pre-rolled and pre-sized, carried in a sealed tin, each labeled with the wound size they're appropriate for by someone who has made that determination in poor lighting before.
A personal kit for disguise: a few small items — hairpins, a folded cloth for a headcovering, a pot of pigment — that aren't a costume but are enough to make someone look different from a distance.
A small anvil, eight pounds, carried by someone for whom a portable work surface for metalwork is not a luxury but a professional necessity.
A fire kit in a tin: tinder of three types, fire steel, a short candle stub for sustaining flame, and a windbreak cloth for lighting in wind.
A set of cold weather additions: thick socks, a face cover, mittens over gloves, and an extra layer of wool that compresses small enough to not matter until it matters enormously.
A travel desk blotter with a stiffened backing, used for writing on any surface, with an inkwell that won't spill while the desk is in use.
A pair of climbing spikes for ice or compacted earth, worn over boots, worn at the ankle attachment point, suggesting they've been on difficult terrain.
A personal cipher key, one page, laminated against moisture, for use with an encryption system the owner uses in correspondence — and in notes to themselves.
A spare set of clothing, one change, lightweight, rolled tight inside a waterproof bag inside a pack pocket — carried by someone who has been wet and far from dry clothes before.

A complete kit in a single pack that contains, on examination, exactly the right item for each of the last twenty-three situations its owner has faced, with no wasted weight — the result of years of refinement by someone who thinks very carefully about what they carry and why.
